summarization.a: Figure presents DFT-optimized geometries that illustrate how an HfX₄ precursor adsorbs on different oxygen sites of an OH-terminated HfO₂ surface. Three distinct adsorption configurations are shown: attachment to a terminal hydroxyl group (a), a terminal oxygen (b), and a bridging oxygen (c). The atomic species and bonding environment are explicitly visualized to reveal the preferred anchoring mode of the Hf precursor.

d.question: Why is analyzing multiple adsorption sites, as shown in Figure 1, important for understanding precursor behavior in ALD processes?
d.answer: Studying the adsorption geometries on different oxygen sites helps determine how the HfX₄ precursor interacts with the surface, which influences reaction pathways and growth mechanisms in ALD. Each site—terminal OH, terminal O, and bridging O—offers distinct bonding environments that affect precursor activation, ligand exchange, and subsequent film formation. By comparing these configurations, researchers can identify the most favorable anchoring site and optimize surface chemistry for improved deposition efficiency and film quality.

b.question: Which adsorption site does the HfX₄ precursor anchor to in all three panels of Figure 2?

a.question: Does Figure 2 show the optimized adsorption geometries of an HfX₄ precursor on different oxygen sites of the HfO₂ surface?

c.question: What surface features are highlighted in the three adsorption configurations?
c.answer: Terminal hydroxyl group (a), Terminal oxygen (b), Bridging oxygen (c)
